title: All of them or none
checkLabels.t7: You didn't use style on the tag
why.empty: The editor is empty. Bring the file back and start again.
why.untouched: The code is exactly as it was.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
why.unlinked: The page can't find `styles.css`. You've edited `index.html`.
hint2.unlinked: Restore both files and work only in `styles.css`.
why.no-h1: There is no heading. You've edited `index.html`.
why.no-p: There are no paragraphs. You've edited `index.html`.
hint2.h1-not-blue: A rule for `h1` with the `color` property. The value is `darkblue`.
why.p-not-brown: A paragraph is left without colour — or not all of them are brown. The check looks at all three.
hint2.p-not-brown: One rule for `p` catches every paragraph at once. Don't write three.
hint3.p-not-brown: If you tried to colour just one of them — that's exactly the lesson. By tag you can't. Go back and colour all three.
hint4.p-not-brown: `p { color: saddlebrown; }` — one rule, three paragraphs.
why.no-gap: The blocks are stuck together. It wants 12 pixels under the heading AND under every paragraph.
hint2.no-gap: The property is `margin-bottom`. Both `h1` and `p` need it.
hint3.no-gap: You can write it twice, in both rules. Or once: `h1, p { margin-bottom: 12px; }` — the comma means "both of these". The check accepts either, because the result is the same.
hint4.no-gap: `margin-bottom: 12px` on the heading and on the paragraphs.
why.used-inline: You used `style` on the tag. I understand why — you wanted to reach just one paragraph. There's no way, not with what you know. Remove it.
hint2.used-inline: That's the whole point of this lesson: a tag selector can't tell one paragraph from another. The tool comes next time.
hint3.used-inline: The attribute works, but it's a step backwards — it put you back where you were in lesson 01. Three elements, three copies of the same thing.
hint4.used-inline: Delete the `style` attribute. Make all three paragraphs the same and accept that the third one can't be different yet.
why.lost-p: A paragraph is gone. The work is in `styles.css`.
blocks.1.text: You've known the tag selector since lesson 02. `p` catches the paragraphs, `h1` catches the heading. You'll meet it under three names — *type selector*, *tag name selector*, *element selector*. Same thing.
blocks.2.text: It has a widest relative too: `*` picks every element on the page, no exceptions. That's where almost every reset like the one in lesson 05 begins. Now you'll see how far the whole family reaches.
blocks.4.text: The cafe's menu has three lines. Dress it:
blocks.5.items.2: Under the heading and under every paragraph — 12 pixels of space
blocks.6.text: The third one is wanted by both `h1` and `p`. You can write it twice. Or once, with a comma between the two selectors: `h1, p { }`. The result is identical and the check takes either — the comma just saves typing.
blocks.7.text: Before you submit, do one more thing: try to make ONLY the last paragraph — the one about Christmas — a different colour. Seriously, try.
blocks.9.text: You write `p` and catch all of them. You write `p` again — you catch all of them again, only now you've overwritten the first rule. There's no way to say "the third one", because a tag selector doesn't know there is a third. It knows what a thing IS, not which one it is.
blocks.10.text: The only way out, with what you have, is to go back to the `style` attribute on the tag. It works. And it puts you exactly where you were in lesson 01 — three elements, three repetitions, nothing learned.
blocks.11.text: That's why this check rejects it. Not because it's forbidden, but because it isn't the answer.
blocks.13.text: What you're missing is a way to PUT A LABEL on one element and then call it by that label. HTML has such a thing — you used it in the HTML course without needing it. CSS is what it was waiting for. The next lesson is about that.
blocks.15.items.0: Type selector, tag name selector, element selector — three names for `p` and `h1`.
blocks.15.items.1: `*` catches every element. That's where every reset begins.
blocks.15.items.2: A comma between two selectors means "both of these". One rule instead of two.
blocks.15.items.3: A type selector knows WHAT a thing is, not WHICH one it is. All the paragraphs or none.
blocks.16.text: That isn't a shortcoming of CSS. It's a division of labour: the type comes from the HTML, and which one comes from something you attach yourself.
walkthrough.0.text: `h1` and `p` are two separate rules, because their colours differ. A comma is no help here — it gives the same style to both selectors.
walkthrough.1.text: `margin-bottom` is the same for both, so I collected it into `h1, p { }`. If you wrote it twice, in the two rules, the result is exactly the same and the check passes. The comma is a convenience, not a rule.
walkthrough.2.text: Notice what I did NOT do: I didn't touch `body`. The file was already styling it and that isn't this lesson's job. Don't rewrite things that work just because they're in front of you.
walkthrough.3.text: The third paragraph is the same colour as the other two. The attempt to single it out was deliberate and had to fail. If you ended up reaching for `style` on the tag and got annoyed — the lesson did exactly its job.
